title: Do not hardcode secrets or long-lived credentials
text: Hardcoded tokens, passwords, API keys, and similar credentials create rotation, exposure, and audit problems. Prefer runtime-provided secrets with narrowly scoped access.
summary: Secret-like literals should live in secret management or deployment configuration, not in source.
embedding_text:
rule_no: SEC.1
pack_id: shared_security
language_id: shared
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: SEC
title: Do not hardcode secrets or long-lived credentials
summary: Secret-like literals should live in secret management or deployment configuration, not in source.
keywords: not, secrets, credentials, sec.1, hardcode, long-lived, secret-like, literals, should, live, secret, management
category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: hardcoded_secret
fix_guidance: Move the secret into environment-backed configuration or a secret manager and inject it at runtime.
text: Hardcoded tokens, passwords, API keys, and similar credentials create rotation, exposure, and audit problems. Prefer runtime-provided secrets with narrowly scoped access.
fix_guidance: Move the secret into environment-backed configuration or a secret manager and inject it at runtime.

title: Build shell commands from trusted primitives, not concatenated strings
text: Command construction through string concatenation is fragile and exposes injection risk. Prefer argument vectors, explicit allow-lists, or dedicated APIs.
summary: Shell execution paths should not interpolate untrusted fragments into a command string.
embedding_text:
rule_no: SEC.2
pack_id: shared_security
language_id: shared
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: SEC
title: Build shell commands from trusted primitives, not concatenated strings
summary: Shell execution paths should not interpolate untrusted fragments into a command string.
keywords: shell, not, command, sec.2, build, commands, trusted, primitives, concatenated, strings, execution, paths
category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: shell_injection
fix_guidance: Use structured subprocess or exec APIs that separate the executable from validated arguments.
text: Command construction through string concatenation is fragile and exposes injection risk. Prefer argument vectors, explicit allow-lists, or dedicated APIs.
fix_guidance: Use structured subprocess or exec APIs that separate the executable from validated arguments.

title: Parameterize SQL instead of composing query text dynamically
text: SQL should be parameterized through the database driver's bind facilities or a higher-level abstraction. Dynamic text assembly should be reserved for carefully controlled structural clauses.
summary: Query text built from variables is difficult to reason about and easy to misuse.
embedding_text:
rule_no: SEC.3
pack_id: shared_security
language_id: shared
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: SEC
title: Parameterize SQL instead of composing query text dynamically
summary: Query text built from variables is difficult to reason about and easy to misuse.
keywords: text, sql, query, should, sec.3, parameterize, instead, composing, dynamically, built, variables, difficult
category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: sql_injection, dynamic_sql
fix_guidance: Bind values separately from the SQL string and constrain structural query changes through allow-listed fragments.
text: SQL should be parameterized through the database driver's bind facilities or a higher-level abstraction. Dynamic text assembly should be reserved for carefully controlled structural clauses.
fix_guidance: Bind values separately from the SQL string and constrain structural query changes through allow-listed fragments.

title: Do not leave fallback secrets or test credentials in production code paths
text: Even when intended only for local development or bootstrap, embedded fallback secrets create a second and weaker trust path that is easy to forget during incident response or key rotation.
summary: Backup credentials often become the least-audited credential in the system.

language_id: shared
context_id:

keywords: fallback, secrets, credentials, sec.4, not, leave, test, production, code, paths, backup, often
category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: hardcoded_secret
fix_guidance: Remove embedded fallback credentials and require runtime configuration or a dedicated local-only development path instead.
text: Even when intended only for local development or bootstrap, embedded fallback secrets create a second and weaker trust path that is easy to forget during incident response or key rotation.
fix_guidance: Remove embedded fallback credentials and require runtime configuration or a dedicated local-only development path instead.

title: Keep command execution APIs structured so the executable and arguments remain distinct
text: Separating the executable from validated arguments narrows the injection surface and makes the trust boundary visible in code review across languages and runtimes.
summary: Security review is much stronger when command construction never depends on ad hoc shell parsing.

category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: shell_injection
fix_guidance: Use structured process APIs, validate each argument according to its contract, and avoid passing shell fragments through string concatenation.
text: Separating the executable from validated arguments narrows the injection surface and makes the trust boundary visible in code review across languages and runtimes.
fix_guidance: Use structured process APIs, validate each argument according to its contract, and avoid passing shell fragments through string concatenation.

title: Dynamic query structure should stay allow-listed even when values are parameterized
text: ORDER BY fragments, table identifiers, and optional clauses can still create injection or correctness risk when the allowed structures are not tightly bounded.
summary: Parameter binding protects values, not uncontrolled structural SQL changes.

category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: dynamic_sql
fix_guidance: Keep structural variation behind a small allow-list and continue binding values separately from the SQL text.
text: ORDER BY fragments, table identifiers, and optional clauses can still create injection or correctness risk when the allowed structures are not tightly bounded.
fix_guidance: Keep structural variation behind a small allow-list and continue binding values separately from the SQL text.

title: Example, bootstrap, and fallback credentials in committed code should be treated as real secrets unless clearly impossible to activate
text: Backup and bootstrap secrets often become the least-audited authentication path in a system because everyone assumes they are temporary or unreachable.
summary: "Test" or "dev" labels do not remove rotation, misuse, or confusion risk once credentials are committed.

keywords: bootstrap, credentials, secrets, sec.7, example, fallback, committed, code, should, treated, real, unless
category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: hardcoded_secret
fix_guidance: Remove committed fallback credentials or move them into an isolated local-only development path that cannot ship as a production default.
text: Backup and bootstrap secrets often become the least-audited authentication path in a system because everyone assumes they are temporary or unreachable.

title: Bind closeable resources to try-with-resources when possible
text: Try-with-resources makes the cleanup path visible and reliable, especially when additional failure handling is added later.
summary: Explicitly opened resources should usually be owned by lexical scope.

category: resource_management
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: try_without_resources
fix_guidance: Wrap the resource-producing statement in try-with-resources unless ownership is intentionally transferred elsewhere.
text: Try-with-resources makes the cleanup path visible and reliable, especially when additional failure handling is added later.
fix_guidance: Wrap the resource-producing statement in try-with-resources unless ownership is intentionally transferred elsewhere.

title: Prefer explicit absence modeling over null returns
text: Public Java APIs should prefer Optional, empty collections, exceptions, or documented sentinel objects when absence is part of the contract.
summary: Returning null weakens the contract at exactly the point the caller needs certainty.

category: type_usage
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: null_return
fix_guidance: Return Optional or another explicit absence representation when callers can reasonably continue.
text: Public Java APIs should prefer Optional, empty collections, exceptions, or documented sentinel objects when absence is part of the contract.
fix_guidance: Return Optional or another explicit absence representation when callers can reasonably continue.

title: Return empty collections or explicit optionals instead of nullable container contracts
text: A method that sometimes returns null and sometimes returns a usable container pushes unnecessary uncertainty into every caller branch and encourages defensive clutter.
summary: Absence is easier to consume when the API chooses one deliberate representation.

category: type_usage
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: null_return
fix_guidance: Prefer an empty collection, Optional, or another single absence contract that callers can handle consistently.
text: A method that sometimes returns null and sometimes returns a usable container pushes unnecessary uncertainty into every caller branch and encourages defensive clutter.
fix_guidance: Prefer an empty collection, Optional, or another single absence contract that callers can handle consistently.

title: Keep closeable ownership inside the smallest practical lexical scope
text: Java resource handling becomes harder to review when ownership is transferred across long methods or hidden behind later cleanup code instead of a nearby try-with-resources block.
summary: Resource lifetime should be visible where the resource is opened, used, and released.

category: resource_management
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: try_without_resources
fix_guidance: Open the resource in a small scope and bind it to try-with-resources unless a separate owner is part of the design.
text: Java resource handling becomes harder to review when ownership is transferred across long methods or hidden behind later cleanup code instead of a nearby try-with-resources block.
fix_guidance: Open the resource in a small scope and bind it to try-with-resources unless a separate owner is part of the design.

summary: String-concatenated SQL is hard to review for correctness and security.

title: Be explicit about synchronized collection iteration guarantees
text: Code that shares synchronized collections should still make iteration, compound actions, and external synchronization expectations obvious.
summary: A synchronized wrapper is not the same as holistic thread-safety.

category: thread_safety
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: synchronized_collection
fix_guidance: Document or enforce the synchronization boundary for reads plus writes, not just individual calls.
text: Code that shares synchronized collections should still make iteration, compound actions, and external synchronization expectations obvious.
fix_guidance: Document or enforce the synchronization boundary for reads plus writes, not just individual calls.

title: Keep SQL structure parameterized even when optional clauses are involved
text: Optional filters, ordering, and search criteria are common enough that ad hoc string concatenation looks convenient. The safer project default is still to keep values bound separately and structural choices narrowly controlled.
summary: String-built query variants tend to grow into correctness and injection problems over time.

trigger_patterns: string_sql_concatenation
fix_guidance: Build the query through prepared statements or a constrained builder that keeps values parameterized.
text: Optional filters, ordering, and search criteria are common enough that ad hoc string concatenation looks convenient. The safer project default is still to keep values bound separately and structural choices narrowly controlled.
fix_guidance: Build the query through prepared statements or a constrained builder that keeps values parameterized.

title: Synchronized wrappers still need explicit locking for iteration and compound actions
text: Iteration, check-then-act flows, and multi-step updates can still race even when the collection wrapper synchronizes single operations.
summary: Individual synchronized calls do not automatically make higher-level collection logic safe.

category: concurrency
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: synchronized_collection
fix_guidance: Guard the surrounding iteration or compound action with the correct lock or use a collection with semantics that match the access pattern.
text: Iteration, check-then-act flows, and multi-step updates can still race even when the collection wrapper synchronizes single operations.
fix_guidance: Guard the surrounding iteration or compound action with the correct lock or use a collection with semantics that match the access pattern.

title: Broad Exception catches should be justified by a real recovery boundary
text: Application code should catch the narrowest exception family it can actually recover from. A broad catch belongs only at a boundary that can translate failures coherently.
summary: Catching Exception often hides cancellation, programming errors, or contract drift that callers should still see.

category: error_handling
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: catch_exception
fix_guidance: Catch the specific exception types you intend to recover from, and rethrow or translate unexpected failures at a well-defined boundary.
text: Application code should catch the narrowest exception family it can actually recover from. A broad catch belongs only at a boundary that can translate failures coherently.
fix_guidance: Catch the specific exception types you intend to recover from, and rethrow or translate unexpected failures at a well-defined boundary.

title: Prefer managed executors or structured ownership over raw Thread creation in application code
text: Direct thread creation is often a sign that concurrency ownership has not been integrated into the application's existing scheduling or shutdown model.
summary: A new thread introduces lifetime, cancellation, and error-routing questions that should rarely be hidden in a local patch.

title: Prefer constructor injection over Spring field injection
text: Spring dependencies are easier to audit when construction makes required collaborators explicit and immutable by default. Field injection tends to hide wiring requirements and weakens local invariants.
summary: Field injection hides required dependencies and makes test setup and lifecycle assumptions harder to review.

category: configuration
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: spring_field_injection
fix_guidance: Prefer constructor injection with final fields so dependency requirements stay explicit and testable.
text: Spring dependencies are easier to audit when construction makes required collaborators explicit and immutable by default. Field injection tends to hide wiring requirements and weakens local invariants.
fix_guidance: Prefer constructor injection with final fields so dependency requirements stay explicit and testable.

title: Keep transaction ownership out of controller boundaries
text: A controller already owns transport concerns such as request parsing and response shape. Mixing that layer with transaction ownership makes retries, side effects, and service boundaries harder to reason about.
summary: HTTP entrypoints should rarely own the transactional contract directly.
embedding_text:

language_id: java
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: JAVASPRING

summary: HTTP entrypoints should rarely own the transactional contract directly.
keywords: transaction, ownership, controller, boundaries, java.spring.2, keep, out, http, entrypoints, should, rarely, own
category: resource_management
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: spring_transaction_on_controller
fix_guidance: Move transactional work into a service boundary that owns business-level consistency rather than the HTTP adapter.
text: A controller already owns transport concerns such as request parsing and response shape. Mixing that layer with transaction ownership makes retries, side effects, and service boundaries harder to reason about.
fix_guidance: Move transactional work into a service boundary that owns business-level consistency rather than the HTTP adapter.

title: ConfigurationProperties bindings should pair with explicit validation
text: Spring configuration binding turns external text into typed application state. That boundary is easier to trust when validation rules are explicit and fail fast during startup.
summary: Typed configuration is safer when invalid startup state is rejected close to binding time.

reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: spring_configuration_properties
fix_guidance: Add explicit validation for bound properties and keep required constraints obvious at the binding boundary.
text: Spring configuration binding turns external text into typed application state. That boundary is easier to trust when validation rules are explicit and fail fast during startup.
fix_guidance: Add explicit validation for bound properties and keep required constraints obvious at the binding boundary.

title: Request-path findAll calls should make data volume and DTO boundaries explicit
text: A controller path that calls findAll is easy to write but frequently under-specifies response size, loading shape, and domain-to-DTO boundaries. Review the request contract before turning repository-wide reads into API output.
summary: Exposing repository-wide reads directly from a request path often hides pagination, mapping, or N+1 risks.
